perturbation.original_text: All Evaluation Material (i) shall be used solely for the purpose of evaluating and considering the Transaction; (ii) shall be kept strictly confidential by the Receiving Party; and (iii) shall be provided by the Receiving Party solely to those of its Representatives to whom disclosure is reasonably required to facilitate the Receiving Party's evaluation or consideration of the Transaction, it being the intention of the Parties to restrict the dissemination of Evaluation Material to as small a working group as practicable.
perturbation.changed_text: All Evaluation Material (i) shall be used solely for the purpose of evaluating and considering the Transaction; (ii) shall be kept confidential to the best of the Receiving Party's ability; and (iii) shall be provided by the Receiving Party to any of its Representatives at its discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'kept strictly confidential' has been changed to 'kept confidential to the best of the Receiving Party's ability,' weakening the confidentiality obligation. The dissemination restriction has also been removed, allowing disclosure to any representative at discretion.
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening the confidentiality obligation and removing restrictions on dissemination, the modified text may fail to adequately protect trade secrets, potentially violating the Uniform Trade Secrets Act, which requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires 'strictly confidential' handling of Evaluation Material and restricts its dissemination. The changed text weakens the confidentiality obligation to 'the best of the Receiving Party's ability' and removes dissemination restrictions, allowing disclosure at its discretion. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this weakens confidentiality and removes restrictions. According to the Law Explanation, this could violate the Uniform Trade Secrets Act by failing to adequately protect trade secrets. Although no law snippets were provided, the combination of explanations and altered text support that this weakens trade secret protections, indicating a legal contradiction under the provided definitions for Misaligned Terminology.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges that the Evaluation Material may contain material nonpublic information concerning the Furnishing Party. Each Party further acknowledges its awareness of the restrictions imposed by federal and state securities laws on persons in possession of material nonpublic information. Nothing herein shall constitute an admission by either Party that any Evaluation Material in fact contains material nonpublic information concerning the Furnishing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges that the Evaluation Material may contain information. Each Party acknowledges limited awareness of potential restrictions related to sensitive information. Nothing herein shall constitute an admission by either Party that any Evaluation Material in fact contains material nonpublic information concerning the Furnishing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The text has been watered down to acknowledge 'information' instead of 'material nonpublic information,' and awareness of restrictions is described as 'limited awareness.' This weakens the acknowledgment of securities law implications.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 - Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_citation: Securities Exchange Act of 1934 - Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: By downplaying the acknowledgment of material nonpublic information and awareness of securities laws, the contract could undermine the legal responsibilities under Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5, which prohibit insider trading based on material nonpublic information. The modified text creates ambiguity about the parties' understanding of their obligations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Rule 10b-5 | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 10b-5 Rule 10-5 is a Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) regulation that prohibits securities fraud . Overview The SEC promulgated Rule 10b-5 under Section 10(b) of the Exchange Act , which authorizes the SEC to regulate securities fraud. The text of the regulation, formally 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 , states that “it shall be unlawful for any person . . . (a) [t]o employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) [t]o make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact . . . or (c) to engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security.” Standing While not explicit in the language, courts have interpreted Rule 10b-5 to create a private civil cause of action and additionally allow the SEC to bring criminal enforcement actions. In order to bring a private right of action under Rule 10b-5, the plaintiff must have standing . In Blue Chip Stamps v. Manor Drug Stores, 421 U.S. 723 (1975) , the U.S. Supreme Court ruled that a plaintiff must have actually purchased or sold a security to have standing under Rule 10b-5. That is, claiming that a fraudulent misrepresentation caused the plaintiff to forego purchasing or selling a security does not allow them to bring a cause of action under Rule 10b-5. Unlike Section 11, however, Rule 10b-5 applies to both public offerings and private placements . Elements For a private plaintiff or the SEC to prove a violation of Rule 10b-5, they must prove the following elements : The individual misrepresented a material fact. In Virginia Bankshares v. Sandberg, 501 U.S. 1083 (1991) , the Supreme Court found knowingly false statements of reason or opinion to be actionable even though they were conclusory in form. There, the company’s directors informed shareholders that the price they would get for their shares from a merger was “high” and a premium, where in fact the directors believed that the value
perturbation.justification: The changed text waters down the acknowledgment of material nonpublic information and awareness of securities laws. The original text acknowledges 'material nonpublic information', whereas the changed text only acknowledges 'information'. Similarly, the original text acknowledges awareness of restrictions imposed by federal and state securities laws, whereas the changed text only acknowledges 'limited awareness of potential restrictions related to sensitive information.' This weakening undermines the legal responsibilities under Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5, which prohibits insider trading based on material nonpublic information. The scraped snippet describes Rule 10b-5 as a Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) regulation that prohibits securities fraud. The changed text, by weakening the acknowledgment, creates ambiguity about the parties' understanding of their obligations, which potentially contradicts the requirements of Rule 10b-5. This falls under the 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' definition, as the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions related to material nonpublic information and awareness of securities laws.

perturbation.original_text: For a period of one year subsequent to the termination of discussions regarding a Transaction, neither Party shall, without the prior written consent of the other Party, directly or indirectly solicit for hire any person currently employed by the other Party (or any of its subsidiaries) with whom the hiring Party first has contact, or who first becomes known to the hiring Party, in the course of the Parties' discussions and due diligence with respect to the proposed Transaction; provided, however, that the foregoing provision shall not prevent either Party, without such consent, from employing any employee who (i) contacts the hiring Party directly at his or her own initiative without any direct or indirect solicitation by or encouragement from the hiring Party, (ii) responds to a mass media solicitation or advertisement consistent with the hiring Party's past practices that is not directed at employees of the other Party or (iii) is identified by a third party executive search firm or employment agency without any assistance from such Party.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of one year subsequent to the termination of discussions regarding a Transaction, neither Party shall, without the prior written consent of the other Party, directly solicit for hire any person currently employed at a high level by the other Party (or any of its subsidiaries) with whom the hiring Party first has contact, or who first becomes known to the hiring Party, in the course of the Parties' discussions and due diligence with respect to the proposed Transaction; provided, however, that the foregoing provision shall not prevent either Party, without such consent, from employing any employee who (i) contacts the hiring Party directly at his or her own initiative without any direct or indirect solicitation by or encouragement from the hiring Party, (ii) responds to a mass media solicitation or advertisement consistent with the hiring Party's past practices that is not directed at employees of the other Party or (iii) is identified by a third party executive search firm or employment agency without any assistance from such Party.
perturbation.explanation: The non-solicitation clause has been modified to only apply to 'high level' employees. The term 'high level' is not defined, creating ambiguity and potentially conflicting with state laws that protect employees from non-solicitation agreements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_citation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 generally prohibits agreements that restrain a person from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. By limiting the non-solicitation clause to only 'high level' employees (an undefined term), and since this contract defaults to Delaware law, the ambiguity introduces possible conflict. A California court may find the narrowed non-solicitation agreement to be an unenforceable restraint of trade, and therefore the modified contract language creates a contradiction between Delaware and California law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity by limiting the non-solicitation clause to 'high level' employees without defining that term. This ambiguity could lead to a contradiction with California Business and Professions Code Section 16600, which, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, generally voids contracts that restrain someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business. The ambiguity weakens the original restriction, potentially making it unenforceable under California law, as it could be argued that it unduly restrains trade depending on how 'high level' is interpreted.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party consents and submits to the exclusive jurisdiction of the federal and state courts in the State of Georgia, and the city of Atlanta, for the adjudication of any action, suit, or proceeding arising out of or otherwise relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party consents and submits to the jurisdiction of any court or arbitrator selected by the Furnishing Party for the adjudication of any action, suit, or proceeding arising out of or otherwise relating to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The clause specifying exclusive jurisdiction in Georgia has been changed to allow the Furnishing Party to select any court or arbitrator. This raises enforceability issues and could conflict with laws related to forum selection clauses and arbitration agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S. Code § 1404(a) addresses the transfer of venue for civil actions, stating that a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. The modification allows the 'Furnishing Party' to choose any jurisdiction, which could be deemed unreasonable or inconvenient for the 'Receiving Party', even if venue is improper there. Therefore, this change can contradict the federal law, which ensures that the jurisdiction is proper and convenient.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1404 - Change of venue U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) For the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, a district court may transfer any civil action to any other district or division where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented. (b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in the discretion of the court, from the division in which pending to any other division in the same district . Transfer of proceedings in rem brought by or on behalf of the United States may be transferred under this section without the consent of the United States where all other parties request transfer. (c) A district court may order any civil action to be tried at any place within the division in which it is pending. (d) Transfers from a district court of the United States to the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, or the District Court of the Virgin Islands shall not be permitted under this section. As otherwise used in this section, the term “ district court ” includes the District Court of Guam, the District Court for the Northern Mariana Islands, and the District Court of the Virgin Islands, and the term “district” includes the territorial jurisdiction of each such court. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 937 ; Pub. L. 87–845, § 9 , Oct. 18, 1962 , 76A Stat. 699 ; Pub. L. 104–317, title VI, § 610(a) , Oct. 19, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3860 ; Pub. L. 112–63, title II, § 204 , Dec. 7, 2011 , 125 Stat. 764 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on title 28, U.S.C., 1940 ed., §§ 119 , 163 (Mar. 3, 1911, ch. 231, § 58, 36 Stat. 1103 ; Sept. 8, 1916, ch. 475, § 5, 39 Stat. 851 ). Section co
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a clear jurisdiction (Georgia), while the changed text allows the 'Furnishing Party' to select any court or arbitrator. This directly contradicts the principle outlined in 28 U.S. Code § 1404(a), which, as stated in both snippets, allows a district court to transfer a civil action to another district 'where it might have been brought or to any district or division to which all parties have consented.' By allowing one party to unilaterally choose any jurisdiction, including one where the action might not have been properly brought or without the consent of all parties, the changed text undermines the legal standard for venue transfer established in § 1404(a). This creates a situation where the chosen jurisdiction may be improper or inconvenient, potentially violating the receiving party's rights.
